message.content:
The shop smelled of cedar shavings, brass polish, and the faint, metallic tang of old oil. Dust motes danced in the single shaft of afternoon sunlight that pierced the gloom, illuminating hundreds of ticking faces.

Seven-year-old Leo stood on a stool, his chin resting on the glass counter. Before him sat Elias, a man whose fingers were as gnarled as the roots of an oak tree, yet moved with the precision of a spider weaving silk. He was adjusting the escapement of a grandfather clock that had stood silent for twenty years.

"Mr. Elias?" Leo whispered, afraid to disturb the symphony of ticks.

Elias didn’t look up. "Yes, Leo?"

"Why do they all tick at different speeds? The big one goes *tock... tock...* but the little silver one goes *tick-tick-tick-tick*."

Elias finally looked up, his eyes magnified behind thick spectacles. He smiled, a slow cracking of dry earth. "They don’t tick at different speeds, Leo. They tick at the same speed. One second is one second, whether it’s measured by a giant pendulum or a tiny spring."

"But they sound different," Leo insisted. "The big one sounds like it’s walking slowly. The little one sounds like it’s running."

"Ah," Elias said, setting down his tweezers. "That is the trick of time. It doesn’t change, but our perception of it does. Listen."

He picked up a small, intricate pocket watch and held it to Leo’s ear. The ticking was rapid, frantic.

"This watch measures seconds," Elias said. "Fast. Precise. Like a heartbeat when you’re scared."

Then, he pointed to the grandfather clock. Its pendulum swung with a heavy, deliberate grace. *Swish... clack
